narrative_markdown:
## Executive Summary
Clio ranks first, Figma second, and Notion third for AI resilience. The spread is partly execution-driven, but it is also category-driven: regulated legal practice management structurally supports higher trust, accountability, switching cost, and workflow dependence than horizontal knowledge work; design collaboration sits between those extremes.

Clio is the strongest because it owns system-of-record legal workflow data and high-stakes operational outcomes. Stronger models make Clio more useful because the scarce asset is not raw generation, but governed case context, permissions, billing, calendaring, and accountability. Figma is next because it owns real design-system context and has unusually strong agent-facing surfaces through Dev Mode, Code Connect, APIs, plugins, and MCP support. Notion remains credible and important, but it is the most exposed of the three to generic workspace and agent-layer commoditization unless it deepens system-of-action ownership.

Evidence window: current official company materials accessed April 14, 2026, with emphasis on pricing, developer docs, AI and agent features, integrations, and security or trust materials.

## 2x2 Placement
Axes used in both the narrative and visual:
- X-axis: Proprietary Advantage
- Y-axis: Workflow Criticality + AI Resilience Under Stronger Models

| Company | Placement | Rationale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Clio | Upper-right leader | Clio combines regulated workflow context, daily operational dependence, and real accountability. Its AI is attached to case execution and firm operations rather than a generic chat layer. |
| Figma | Upper-right, below Clio | Figma owns design-system context and developer handoff in a way that gets better as models improve, but final production accountability still sits outside Figma. |
| Notion | Right-of-center, below Figma | Notion has broad distribution and strong agent surfaces, but much of its AI value is closer to search, synthesis, and orchestration layers that commodity models and wrappers can reproduce. |

## Full Score Table

### Clio
| Factor | Score | Rationale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Context | 5 | Direct evidence. Clio Manage, Grow, and Manage AI operate on firm matters, intake, billing, calendars, documents, and client records inside one legal workflow. |
| Trust | 5 | Direct evidence. Clio emphasizes legal-specific AI principles, bar-association approval, and enterprise security for sensitive legal data. |
| Distribution | 4 | Direct evidence. Clio sells an end-to-end legal operating stack and exposes 250+ integrations plus APIs into the same buyer. |
| Judgment | 4 | Direct evidence. Manage AI is aimed at legal tasks such as case summaries, deadline extraction, and bill generation, but lawyers remain the final decision maker. |
| Liability and governance | 5 | Direct evidence. Legal work needs auditability and compliance; Clio pairs AI use with security controls, regional hosting, and policy commitments. |
| Proprietary advantage subtotal | 23 | Structural edge comes from regulated context, trust, and legal workflow ownership rather than generic AI UX. |
| Frequency | 5 | Direct evidence. Practice management, intake, calendaring, billing, and document work are daily firm operations. |
| Operational dependence | 5 | Inference. Firms running matters, timekeeping, and payments in Clio are materially impaired if the platform is removed. |
| System position | 5 | Direct evidence. Clio sits as a system of record and system of action for law-firm operations rather than a drafting sidecar. |
| Switching cost | 5 | Inference. Matter history, client records, workflows, payments, and integrations raise migration pain materially. |
| Budget durability | 5 | Inference. Core practice-management spend is hard to cut because it is tied to running the firm and collecting revenue. |
| Workflow criticality subtotal | 25 | This is the deepest operational embed of the three. |
| Model improvement test | 5 | Inference. Better models make Clio's governed legal context more useful because the platform owns the workflow state the models need. |
| Wrapper risk test | 5 | Inference. A standalone legal AI wrapper still needs the matter system, permissions, billing, and audit trail that Clio already owns. |
| Agent readiness test | 4 | Direct evidence. Clio has APIs, OAuth, app-directory distribution, and embedded AI, but its agent surface is less open than Notion's or Figma's. |
| Accountability test | 5 | Direct evidence. The economic and malpractice risk of being wrong stays inside the legal workflow, where Clio is embedded. |
| Outcome depth test | 5 | Direct evidence. The product affects deadlines, intake conversion, bill generation, payments, and case execution, not just document drafts. |
| AI resilience subtotal | 24 | Better models are mostly accretive because Clio owns the workflow and accountability layer. |
| Total | 72 | Strong resilience. |

### Figma
| Factor | Score | Rationale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Context | 4 | Direct evidence. Figma stores design files, components, variables, annotations, design systems, and Dev Mode context used in implementation. |
| Trust | 4 | Direct evidence. Enterprise security, SSO and SCIM, and governance features make Figma a trusted source of design truth, though stakes are lower than legal workflows. |
| Distribution | 4 | Direct evidence. Figma spans designers, PMs, and developers through design, Dev Mode, VS Code extension, plugins, and community surfaces. |
| Judgment | 4 | Inference. Good product and systems design still needs human taste, but Figma owns the artifacts and review loops where that judgment is applied. |
| Liability and governance | 4 | Direct evidence. Governance+, Activity Logs, and Discovery APIs show real admin and compliance depth even if Figma is not the final production authority. |
| Proprietary advantage subtotal | 20 | Figma has meaningful moat layers, but not the same hard accountability profile as Clio. |
| Frequency | 5 | Direct evidence. Design iteration, review, and handoff happen continuously in product teams. |
| Operational dependence | 4 | Inference. Many software teams can operate without Figma only with meaningful collaboration loss and slower handoff. |
| System position | 4 | Direct evidence. Figma is the design-system and handoff hub, but code, tickets, and production telemetry live elsewhere. |
| Switching cost | 4 | Inference. Libraries, variables, prototypes, comments, plugins, and habits create notable but not absolute lock-in. |
| Budget durability | 4 | Inference. Design and dev-collaboration spend is durable for product teams but less non-discretionary than legal operations software. |
| Workflow criticality subtotal | 21 | High recurring embed, but not the deepest operational dependency in the set. |
| Model improvement test | 4 | Direct evidence. MCP support, Dev Mode, Make connectors, and Code Connect make better models more useful when grounded in Figma context. |
| Wrapper risk test | 4 | Inference. Generic design or code wrappers can imitate parts of the surface, but not the collaborative file graph and system context as easily. |
| Agent readiness test | 5 | Direct evidence. Figma explicitly supports MCP, Code Connect, REST APIs, plugins, variables automation, and developer workflows. |
| Accountability test | 3 | Inference. Figma influences shipped UI, but final correctness, compliance, and runtime behavior are validated elsewhere. |
| Outcome depth test | 4 | Direct evidence. Figma sits upstream of shipped product outcomes and design-system consistency, not just inspiration or mockups. |
| AI resilience subtotal | 20 | Model gains are favorable for Figma, but some generative surface area still commoditizes. |
| Total | 61 | Strong resilience. |

### Notion
| Factor | Score | Rationale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Context | 4 | Direct evidence. Notion stores docs, databases, comments, and workspace structure, and Enterprise Search reaches connected apps with citations. |
| Trust | 3 | Direct evidence. Notion has strong security and permissions, but many high-stakes decisions still require verification in source systems. |
| Distribution | 5 | Direct evidence. Notion is horizontally distributed across teams as a workspace, API platform, Enterprise Search layer, and AI surface. |
| Judgment | 3 | Inference. Much of the AI value is synthesis and routing on general models rather than proprietary domain judgment. |
| Liability and governance | 4 | Direct evidence. Custom Agents security features, audit logs, permissions, and prompt-injection mitigations create meaningful governance. |
| Proprietary advantage subtotal | 19 | Broad distribution is strong, but proprietary judgment and hard accountability are thinner than Clio's and somewhat thinner than Figma's. |
| Frequency | 5 | Direct evidence. Teams use docs, project tracking, search, and coordination daily. |
| Operational dependence | 4 | Inference. Notion is embedded in many recurring workflows, but some organizations can substitute other docs or PM tools over time. |
| System position | 3 | Direct evidence. Notion is often a coordination layer above the true systems of record even when Enterprise Search connects them. |
| Switching cost | 3 | Inference. Workspaces can become sticky, but data is comparatively portable and many use cases are template-driven. |
| Budget durability | 4 | Inference. Knowledge-work software budget is durable, but less protected than legal operating systems and easier to consolidate. |
| Workflow criticality subtotal | 19 | Recurring usage is strong, but hard operational dependence is mixed. |
| Model improvement test | 4 | Direct evidence. Enterprise Search, model switching, MCP, and Custom Agents all improve as base models improve. |
| Wrapper risk test | 2 | Inference. Generic AI workspaces and agent layers can reproduce much of the search, drafting, and orchestration value unless Notion owns more execution. |
| Agent readiness test | 5 | Direct evidence. Notion offers MCP, Custom Agents, OAuth APIs, Link Previews, and permission-aware search across connected apps. |
| Accountability test | 3 | Direct evidence. Notion provides audit and permission controls, but ultimate business accountability usually remains in the underlying app or human approver. |
| Outcome depth test | 3 | Inference. Notion increasingly moves toward action, yet much current value is still search, synthesis, documentation, and coordination rather than closed-loop execution. |
| AI resilience subtotal | 17 | Notion benefits from stronger models, but it remains the most exposed to wrapper compression. |
| Total | 55 | Mixed but promising. |

## Top Strengths
| Company | Strengths |
| --- | --- |
| Clio | System-of-record control over regulated legal workflow data and actions; high trust, security, and governance requirements turn AI from novelty into accountable infrastructure; AI value is attached to deadlines, billing, intake, and matter execution. |
| Figma | Deep design-system context via files, components, variables, and Dev Mode; explicit agent and developer interfaces through MCP, Code Connect, APIs, and plugins; cross-functional distribution across design and engineering makes model gains accretive. |
| Notion | Broad horizontal distribution inside team knowledge and coordination workflows; strong agent surfaces through MCP, Custom Agents, Enterprise Search, APIs, and Link Previews; permission-aware search across workspace and connected apps improves as models get better. |

## Main Vulnerabilities
| Company | Vulnerabilities |
| --- | --- |
| Clio | AI upside is concentrated in a vertical market, so expansion is narrower than horizontal platforms; human lawyers remain the final accountable actor, which caps autonomous judgment; if a competitor wins the legal system of record, AI differentiation alone is not enough. |
| Figma | Generative design and prototyping surfaces are increasingly reproducible by cheaper AI tools; Figma is upstream of production systems, so ultimate accountability sits elsewhere; switching costs are meaningful but not as hard as regulated systems of record. |
| Notion | Many AI features sit close to commodity search, drafting, and orchestration layers; Notion often coordinates work above source systems instead of owning the canonical transaction record; high breadth can mask shallow embed at the team or workflow level. |

## Strategic Read
| Company | Durable layers | Commoditizing layers | Next moves |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Clio | Legal case and matter data, billing and calendar workflow state, compliance and trust, end-to-end firm operating stack | Generic legal drafting, simple chat assistants, standalone document summarization | Deepen system-of-action automations, expose governed agent workflows over APIs, and tie AI more tightly to cited legal outcomes and measurable firm performance. |
| Figma | Design-system source of truth, design-to-dev context, plugin and API surface, Dev Mode and Code Connect | One-off mockup generation, basic asset creation, generic code generation from screenshots | Tighten the code and runtime feedback loop, expand governed org context for agents, and defend the developer surface with better round-trip sync and evaluation. |
| Notion | Workspace graph plus permissions, agent interface and permission-aware enterprise search, broad team distribution | Generic note-taking, summarization, draft generation, shallow RAG search | Own more systems-of-action workflows, make agent outputs more auditable and transactional, and push connectors from search into governed write-back execution. |

## Final Verdict
Clio is the most AI-resilient of the three because it owns the most defensible combination of proprietary context, workflow criticality, and accountability. Figma is second because it controls high-value design-system context and has already opened strong model-facing interfaces that turn better models into better implementation leverage. Notion is still a strong product and distribution layer, but it is the most vulnerable to AI commoditization because too much of its incremental AI value can be recreated above generic models and connected data unless it moves deeper into execution.

## Confidence Level
Medium-high. Official evidence is strong on product surface, APIs, integrations, security, and governance. Confidence is lower on private metrics such as attach rate, seat penetration, retention, and revenue mix for AI features, so some workflow depth and switching-cost judgments are inferred from product position rather than internal usage data.

## Evidence Notes
- Clio: current official pricing, AI, integrations, developer, and security materials.
- Figma: current official pricing, security, Dev Mode, Code Connect, and developer API materials.
- Notion: current official pricing, Enterprise Search, MCP, Custom Agents security, API, Link Preview, and security materials.

structured_object.summary: Clio ranks first, Figma second, and Notion third. Clio's legal system-of-record position makes stronger models accretive to a governed workflow. Figma has strong design-system context and unusually good agent-facing interfaces. Notion has broad distribution and strong agent surfaces, but higher wrapper risk because much of its AI value sits closer to generic knowledge-work orchestration.

Notion has broad distribution and strong permission-aware agent features, yet more of its incremental AI value is exposed to commodity search, synthesis, and orchestration layers.

High trust, security, and governance requirements turn AI from novelty into accountable workflow infrastructure.
AI value is attached to deadlines, billing, intake, and matter execution rather than draft generation alone.
Figma: Deep design-system context through files, components, variables, annotations, and Dev Mode.
Explicit model-facing developer surface through MCP, Code Connect, APIs, plugins, and Make connectors.

Strong agent surface through MCP, Custom Agents, Enterprise Search, APIs, and Link Previews.

Clio: AI upside is concentrated in a vertical market, so horizontal expansion is narrower than in general-purpose platforms.
Human lawyers remain the final accountable actor, which caps autonomous judgment.
If another vendor wins the legal system of record, Clio's AI features alone are not enough to defend the account.
Figma: Generative design and prototyping surfaces are increasingly reproducible by cheaper AI tools.
Figma is upstream of production systems, so ultimate accountability sits elsewhere.
Switching costs are meaningful but not as hard as in regulated systems of record.

High breadth can mask shallow embed at the team or workflow level.

Clio: Legal case and matter data with permissions and workflow state.
Billing, calendaring, intake, and payments tied to firm operations.
Compliance, trust, and accountability requirements inside a legal operating stack.
Figma: Design-system source of truth and reusable component graph.
Design-to-dev context in Dev Mode, variables, and Code Connect.
Plugin, API, and enterprise governance surface around product design workflows.
Notion: Workspace graph with permissions, comments, and structured knowledge.
Permission-aware enterprise search and connected-context retrieval.
Broad team distribution as a coordination and agent-access layer.

Clio: Chat-first AI without workflow ownership or audit trail.
Figma: Basic asset creation and styling suggestions.
Generic code generation from screenshots without shared system context.
Notion: Shallow RAG search over connected content.
Draft generation and generic task orchestration.

Clio: Deepen governed system-of-action automations around deadlines, billing, intake, and matter workflows.
Expose more agent-capable workflows through APIs without weakening permissions and auditability.
Tie AI features more tightly to cited legal outcomes and measurable firm performance.
Figma: Tighten the code and runtime feedback loop so Figma remains central after generation.
Expand governed organization context for agents beyond single-file assistance.
Defend the developer surface with stronger round-trip sync, evaluation, and implementation telemetry.
Notion: Own more systems-of-action workflows rather than stopping at search and synthesis.
Make agent outputs more auditable, permission-aware, and transactional.
Push connectors from retrieval into governed write-back execution and approvals.

structured_object.final_verdict: Clio is the strongest AI-resilience target of the three because it owns the most defensible combination of proprietary context, workflow criticality, and accountability. Figma is second because it controls high-value design-system context and has already built strong interfaces that let better models amplify, rather than bypass, its core workflow. Notion is still resilient enough to matter, but it is the most exposed to wrapper compression because much of its incremental AI value can be recreated above generic models and connected data unless it moves deeper into execution.

confidence.reason: Official evidence is strong on product surface, pricing, APIs, integrations, security, and governance. Confidence is lower on private metrics such as attach rate, retention, seat penetration, and revenue mix for AI features, so some switching-cost and dependence judgments are inferred from product position rather than internal cohort data.

comparison.relative_summary: Clio leads because regulated legal workflow naturally creates stronger trust, accountability, system position, and switching cost than the other two categories. Figma is the best non-regulated target here because it owns shared design-system context and already exposes strong model-facing surfaces. Notion remains valuable and broad, but its category is structurally more exposed to wrapper compression because much of the AI surface can be replicated with generic models plus connected data.
key_differences: Clio's edge is mostly category-structural as well as executional: regulated system-of-record workflows naturally score higher on trust, accountability, and switching cost.
Figma beats Notion on owned workflow context and lower wrapper risk because design systems and developer handoff artifacts are harder to abstract away than generic workspace content.
Notion matches or exceeds Figma on agent-readiness breadth, but its generic knowledge-work category leaves more value exposed to commodity model layers and alternative orchestration shells.
